Were You Asked This?
Design a Navigation/Mapping System

Design a navigation and mapping system that allows users to search for locations, get directions between two points, view real-time traffic information, and navigate with turn-by-turn guidance. The system should support multiple transportation modes (driving, walking, public transit) and provide route optimization based on current traffic conditions.

Asked at:

Google

A navigation system is a mapping and directions service like Google Maps or Apple Maps. Users search for places, pick a start and destination, choose a transportation mode (driving, walking, transit), and receive turn-by-turn directions with live ETAs that adapt to traffic and incidents.

Interviewers ask this because it blends graph algorithms, geospatial data, real-time streaming, API design, and mobile considerations. It tests whether you can balance low-latency route computation with freshness from live traffic, handle multi-modal constraints (e.g., public transit schedules), and build a read-heavy, globally available service with sensible caching and data pipelines. You’re expected to decompose the problem, make clear trade-offs, and show how you’d evolve from a simple MVP to a scalable, reliable system.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to request directions between two points and see step-by-step navigation with an ETA.

Users should be able to select a transportation mode (driving, walking, public transit) and receive mode-appropriate routes.

Users should receive real-time updates to routes and ETAs based on live traffic/incidents, including smart re-routing when conditions change.

Users should be able to view alternate routes and compare key attributes (ETA, distance, tolls, transfers).

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you achieve low-latency route computation (<300–500 ms P95) at global scale while keeping results accurate?

How will you ingest, aggregate, and propagate real-time traffic updates so ETAs and re-routing stay fresh without overwhelming clients?

How will you support multiple transportation modes, especially public transit with schedules, transfers, and time-dependent routing?

How will you store, shard, and version geospatial data (map tiles, road graph) so services and clients stay consistent during updates?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Traffic and incident data continuously change ETAs and best paths. You need a streaming pipeline and push mechanisms so users receive timely re-routing and ETA adjustments without polling or overwhelming devices.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Directions APIs are read-heavy with spiky global demand. Caching hot routes/tiles, precomputations, and regional replicas are critical to keep P95 latency low and availability high.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

The system includes multi-stage workflows: map data ingestion and preprocessing, traffic aggregation, and route computation that may combine walking + transit legs. Treating these as composed steps with clear handoffs improves reliability and evolvability.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

An in-memory cache is essential for hot routes, partial paths, and traffic tiles. Redis provides sub-millisecond access, supports geospatial and TTL semantics, and helps dedupe concurrent identical requests to control tail latency.

Read More
Kafka

Traffic telemetry, incident reports, and sensor feeds are high-volume, append-only streams. Kafka offers durable, ordered, partitioned logs so you can scale ingestion by region and replay data when recomputing traffic models.

Read More
Flink

You need real-time aggregation of speeds and congestion (windowed averages, outlier filtering) and enrichment (map-matching). Flink provides exactly-once streaming semantics and stateful operators well-suited for per-edge rolling computations.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Uber

Ride-sharing also requires low-latency geospatial queries, live driver/rider location streams, dynamic ETAs, and re-routing. The ingestion + streaming + caching stack closely parallels a navigation system’s traffic pipeline.

Read
Practice
Local Delivery Service

Local delivery demands route optimization, ETA accuracy, and real-time updates for changing conditions. The read-heavy APIs and regional sharding patterns mirror navigation design trade-offs.

Read
Practice
Strava

Fitness apps rely on map-matching GPS traces to a road/trail network, route planning, and geospatial partitioning. While not focused on traffic, the underlying map data pipelines and caching considerations are similar.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

No clear strategy for real-time traffic ingestion and propagation

Treating traffic as periodic batch updates leads to stale ETAs and poor re-routing. Without a streaming pipeline and controlled push to clients, you either overwhelm devices with polls or deliver outdated guidance.

Ignoring caching and read-scaling for route computation

Computing every route from scratch overloads the routing service and explodes tail latency. You need tile/OD caching, request deduplication, and regional replicas to serve global demand reliably.

Treating all transportation modes the same

Using a single road-graph approach for transit misses schedules, transfers, and time-dependent constraints, producing invalid or slow routes. Mode-specific data models and algorithms are required for correctness and performance.

Build a global route calculation service for cars that accepts two latitude-longitude pairs and returns the time-optimized route between them, considering real-time traffic data sourced primarily from logged-in Google users. The underlying route connections are updated weekly. The system must maintain a low read latency of under 100 ms and prioritize availability over consistency for its service.
